title: The Five Per Cent Price Test Every Australian SME Owner Should Run Before July
linkedin:
Most business owners know they should review their prices. Very few have run the exact arithmetic.

Here is the calculation worth doing before July 1.

Take your annual revenue. Multiply by five per cent. That is the additional revenue a price increase generates. Now ask: how much of that additional revenue becomes additional profit?

Because costs are largely fixed in the short term, most of it does.

A business doing $4 million in revenue with a 15% net margin earns $600k in profit. A five per cent price increase with no volume loss and unchanged overhead produces approximately $200k in additional gross profit. Net profit moves to around $800k.

That is a 33% profit improvement from a 5% pricing move.

The number that surprises most owners is not the size of the improvement. It is how small the price change needs to be to produce it.

The main objection is customer loss. In established B2B service relationships, five per cent rarely costs clients. The ones who push back tend to be the highest maintenance and the lowest margin anyway.

With six days left in the financial year, if pricing has not been reviewed since last July, two years of award wage increases and input cost growth have been absorbed without recovery. That is the quiet margin erosion the EOFY accounts make measurable for the first time.

The analysis is worth completing before July 1, not after another year has accumulated.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
If your prices have not changed since last July, you have quietly absorbed two full rounds of award wage increases in your costs without recovering them in what you charge.

Quick test: take your annual revenue and multiply by 5%. Because most costs are fixed and do not change with price, the majority of that number flows straight to profit.

For a $4 million business, a 5% increase done well adds more than $150,000 to annual profit. Not from new customers. From the ones you already have.

What is holding you back from running the numbers before July 1?

#AustralianBusiness #ProfitabilityAdvice
google:
For owner-led businesses across Brisbane, South East Queensland, and the NSW and Victorian coasts, the financial year closes on June 30. Six days away.

Most of the planning at this time of year focuses on completing work, collecting outstanding invoices, and reviewing what the year delivered. One conversation that tends to get pushed to August or September is pricing.

Here is why it is worth having before July rather than after.

Award wages increased in July 2024 and will increase again from the first full pay period of July 2025. If prices have not moved in the same period, those cost increases have been absorbed by the margin rather than passed through. Over two years, the compounding effect on annual profit is significant, and it only becomes fully visible once the EOFY accounts are complete.

The arithmetic is straightforward. Take your revenue, apply a five per cent increase, and ask what happens to profit when overhead stays flat. For most businesses, the answer is more encouraging than the discomfort of the conversation would suggest.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria to build the cost and margin picture that makes pricing decisions clear rather than instinctive.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
